forecasts.reasoning: The closure of the borders between Benin and Nigeria's Kwara State occurred on May 7, 2025, in direct response to the "Mahmuda" terror group attempting to cross into Benin to escape Nigerian military operations, alongside concerns about arms smuggling. Rather than de-escalating, Benin progressively expanded the border clampdowns throughout May, extending them to Lagos on May 9 and then to Ogun waterways on May 21. Historical precedents for security and terrorism-related border closures in the region (such as Nigeria's own 2019 trade/security closures) demonstrate that they typically last many months or years. Given the active, ongoing nature of the militant threat and the fact that there is only one month left between the knowledge cutoff (May 31, 2025) and the resolution date (July 1, 2025), a reopening within this tight timeframe is vanishingly unlikely. There is no evidence of diplomatic breakthroughs in late May that would suggest an imminent reversal.
